explicit_text: The Committee of 100 I know is not the same as the one involved in the 1898 massacre, though some descendants of those original powerful families are still part of it. Today, this Committee works to attract industry to Wilmington, but back in 1898, it was made up of the wealthiest and most influential families like the Trasks and Bellamys who controlled the city’s daily life and politics. City officials often act as representatives or puppets of this Committee, which still wields significant power over Wilmington’s affairs.

explicit_text: Wilmington is the city where all these events and power struggles took place, especially during the 1898 insurrection and the 1971 uprising. It’s a place controlled by influential families and committees, and it’s where the racial tensions and violence unfolded, including incidents on streets like Castle Street and Dock Street. The city’s black community had to organize and defend themselves during times of unrest, particularly in the early 1970s.

explicit_text: The Trask family has been one of the most powerful and influential families in Wilmington, with a history of controlling the city’s industry and politics. I know that Trask himself has even stopped industries from coming into Wilmington, showing the family's continued influence. They were part of the original Committee of 100 that dominated Wilmington’s affairs back in 1898, and their descendants still hold sway today.

explicit_text: Standard Oil was once owned by families like the Sprunts and the Kings, who are part of Wilmington’s elite. These families have been influential behind the scenes, even though you don’t hear much about them publicly. Their connection to Standard Oil shows the economic power some of these families hold in the city.

explicit_text: The 1898 Wilmington insurrection was a violent event where poor whites initiated attacks against the black community, fueled by racial hatred and a desire to maintain white supremacy. The Committee of 100, made up of the city’s most powerful families, controlled Wilmington then and orchestrated much of the racial order. This event set a precedent for racial violence and control that echoed into later uprisings.
implicit_text: The violent event in 1898 involved poor whites initiating attacks against the black community, fueled by racial hatred and a desire to maintain white supremacy. The powerful families who controlled the city then orchestrated much of the racial order. This event set a precedent for racial violence and control that echoed into later uprisings.

explicit_text: The 1971 Wilmington uprising was a tense and violent time when the black community had to defend itself against attacks from white residents and police. It started around February 5th, with shootings on Castle Street and Dock Street, and escalated with barricades set up around the church to protect the students and residents. We had to treat injuries ourselves because going to the hospital was risky, and the police were often hostile, even trying to sneak up on us and shoot.

explicit_text: The ROWP was a vigilante organization made up mostly of poor white people from neighborhoods like Sunset Park and Winter Park. Their purpose was to maintain the white order and keep black people 'in their place,' resisting any social progress or demands for equality. They were one of the groups that initiated violence during both the 1898 insurrection and the 1971 uprising.

explicit_text: Sunset Park was one of the neighborhoods where many members of the ROWP came from. It was a place associated with poor white residents who were involved in sustaining the racial order through violence and intimidation. This area played a role in the racial conflicts in Wilmington, especially during the 1971 uprising.

explicit_text: Castle Street was one of the locations in Wilmington where shootings occurred during the 1971 uprising. White attackers would drive through the black neighborhood and shoot at individuals walking alone, creating a climate of fear. It was part of the violent environment we had to defend ourselves against during those days.

These incidents contributed to the community’s need to organize and protect themselves.

explicit_text: Mike's Grocery was a location involved in the events of February 6th, 1971, the night the store was burned down. I know that some of the Wilmington Ten were not present that night, and many of us were actually at the Templeton house instead. The burning of Mike's Grocery was part of the larger unrest during the uprising.

explicit_text: The Templeton house was a refuge during the 1971 uprising where many of us stayed, especially on the night Mike's Grocery was burned. It was a place where Ben and Chili were present, and we organized ourselves to protect the community. The Templeton house was central to our efforts to survive and resist the violence around us.

explicit_text: Fifth Street was one of the streets near the church where a white man was sniping at us during the 1971 uprising. Despite calls to the police and sheriff, they did nothing to stop him, which made us feel like the authorities were siding with the attackers. It was a dangerous place that showed how little protection we had from law enforcement.

explicit_text: Nun Street intersected with Fifth Street, where the white sniper was shooting at us during the 1971 uprising. This area was part of the black neighborhood under attack, and the police repeatedly failed to intervene despite our calls. It was a tense and frightening place during those days.

explicit_text: Herb Butler was a man around fifty years old who used to be a good friend of my father's, but during the 1971 uprising, he told us to let the white folks burn down the church because they had insurance on it. That kind of attitude made us lose respect for him. He was there on Saturday morning, February 6th, when the shooting was happening, and his words showed a lack of solidarity with our struggle.

explicit_text: During the 1971 uprising, we avoided taking injured people to Community Hospital because black folks who went there were often arrested or pressured by the police. We had to treat wounds ourselves with whatever supplies we could find, like alcohol and gauze from neighbors. The hospital was not a safe place for us at that time due to police hostility.
